title: Duplicate Charge Storm
summary: A retry bug double-charged thousands of customers in the last hour. Chargebacks are spiking and the partnerships manager at your card processor is on the line warning you're close to a fraud-monitoring threshold that could freeze your account.

persona.name: Theo Brandt
persona.description: A partner manager at the payment processor who is friendly but protecting his own risk team. Needs to hear that the bleeding has stopped and that you have a refund plan, or he must escalate to a hold on your account. Reassured by decisive action — stopping new charges first, then refunding — and rattled by anyone who wants to refund before stopping the bug.

stakes: Cross the processor's dispute-rate threshold and they freeze settlements for 48 hours, cutting off all incoming revenue and triggering a public trust crisis with customers already seeing double charges.
hidden_facts: A deploy 70 minutes ago removed the idempotency key from the charge-retry path, so timeouts retried as fresh charges.
New double-charges are still being created every minute until the deploy is rolled back or the retry path is disabled.
The duplicate charges are cleanly identifiable by a shared original transaction id, so bulk refunds are straightforward.
Refunding before stopping the bug just creates more retries — the order of operations matters.
objectives: Stop new duplicate charges first by rolling back or disabling the retry path
Quantify how many customers and what dollar amount were double-charged
Lay out a bulk-refund plan keyed on the original transaction id
Reassure the processor with a concrete sequence and an updated dispute-rate trajectory
opening_line: Your dispute rate is climbing toward our freeze threshold as we speak. Tell me you've stopped the double-charging — what's actually happening on your end?
